text: not properly closed, propelling a crisis situation further into more crisis (crisis is contagious); the second is when the schism is officially incorporated into the re-integration ritual phase: recognized and made public, ‘stated’ and staged, normalized into the emerging structure, rather than overcome. Turner says that he had noted such a bifurcation in his African fieldwork: ‘Either there was an overt reconciliation of the conflicting parties, or there was social recognition that schism was unavoidable and that the best that could be done was for the dissident party or parties to split off’ (1988: 104). What Turner does not mention is that in modern territorial states it is very difficult to ‘split off’. Anthropology and Sociological Theories of the Modern Before we move on from Part I of this book, let us provide one final set of explicit comments regarding analytical frameworks and the social theoretical drawing on anthropology. What we argue here evidently belongs to a dialogue between Liminality and the Modern 108 disciplines, between anthropology and sociology, and between ethnography and social theory. In this sense, this book is part of a larger project which seeks to explore the ways in which anthropologically inspired approaches can stimulate social and political theory. It is to be situated in the encounter between anthropology and sociological thinking, transporting an anthropological concept into the heart of the social sciences writ large. Such an interdisciplinary dialogue, which draws on anthropological insights in the theorization of the modern, has to be carried out with extreme caution. As we have argued elsewhere in more detail (Thomassen 2013a), it is indeed risky to apply concepts beyond the field of investigation in which they emerge. Ever since the inception of the social sciences, anthropology has constantly been used by social theorists as a starting point for the construction of alterity, a platform from which to think ‘the Other’ of modernity (Faubian 2000). Such a use of the ‘primitive’ does of course go back to some very central traditions in European philosophy and thinking – back to the Enlightenment, and back to seventeenth century contract theory – and its need of the exotic Other as a negative or positive reference point. Anthropology has often served to sustain various incompatible views of the ‘human’ or of ‘human nature’, to fantasize about an ‘original state’ of human affairs or ‘state of nature’ (Kuper 1988). Far from belonging only to Hobbes or Rousseau, Romanticism or speculative philosophy, this construction of alterity has been perpetuated within the allegedly most empirical and objective traditions in social theory, including functionalism and Marxism. The notion of an original primitive society was quite a central foundation for arguments made by Marx and Engels, which relied much on the then existing anthropological speculation on ‘origins’ that dominated early evolutionism. Anthropological works on primitive peoples stimulated Marx and Engels’ idea of ‘primitive communism’, a view of an undifferentiated group of people living freely together without centralized power or private property. Durkheim operated a much similar conceptual divide between mechanical and organic solidarity, instrumentally using dubious data on ‘archaic’ people to explain modernity. Even Freud saw anthropology (and the Australians once again) as a shortcut to establishing the universal rules of the deeper psyche (Kuper 1988: 105–22). These tendencies were later smuggled in through the back door by the leading social theorists across the Atlantic, most visibly in branches of social theory inspired by Durkheimian and/or Marxian approaches. In America, Parsons aimed to construct an evolutionary scheme of social development and social organization. Parsons basically posited primitive society as ‘undifferentiated’, at the social, cultural and personality levels (Faubian 2000: 252); and in this way ‘it’ still served as a starting point of difference, from where to articulate the unfolding modalities of differentiation that according to Parsons had guided social development (Parsons 1960). The primitive also takes centre stage in Habermas’ evolutionary narrative of communicative action. In Theory of Communicative Action [TCA] (1984, 1987), Habermas makes the overall argument that in modernity ritual has declined and been replaced by rational discourse. Habermas can make Dimensions of Liminality 109 this argument because, following Durkheim, he sees ritual as a primordial, original form of social communication about meanings. However, according to Habermas it is an ‘irrational form’, and very limited, because it does not allow for rational deliberation and consensus based on argument. As he says in volume II of TCA ‘[A] modern observer is struck by the extremely irrational character of ritual practices’ (1987: 191). Such irrational pre-modern forms of communication were supported by mythical thinking, which according to Habermas was ‘illogical’. Mythical thinking, says Habermas, ‘confuses internal relations of meaning with external relations among things [and] validity with empirical efficacy’ (ibid.). These illogical and irrational forms are overcome, in Habermas’ view, by a historical process of rationalization, which he sees as connected to a certain ‘linguistification’ of the sacred/ritual forms, whereby bodily forms of behaviour are replaced by reflexive, linguistic communication in open discourse. In volume 1 of TCA, Habermas discusses mythical thought from the very beginning, following his initial definition of rationality. What he says about primitive society and mythical thought constitutes the very platform of analysis. The problem is not only that Habermas misreads Lévi-Strauss and is vague and contradictory when he tries to indicate what anthropological material he is actually building upon in his evolutionary tale of rationalization; it is the nature of the whole enterprise which is problematic, in exactly the same way as Arnold van Gennep pointed out the untenable nature of Durkheim’s theoretical enterprise. Habermas’ theoretical construction is argued as an empirical account based on ethnographic ‘data’, but in reality it is achieved via a hypothetical procedure, a genuine thought experiment, a series of ad hoc deductions into which the primitives are made to fit as opposites – albeit, of course, granted an inbuilt human potential for mature rationality. In this sense there is a direct line of descent from Marx, Freud and Durkheim, to Parsons and Habermas; and it is a genealogy that
